message.content:
For a launch audit, **CrUX** should be treated as your **field-data gate**: it reports real-user performance for the Core Web Vitals (**LCP**, **INP**, **CLS**) and also includes other user-experience metrics such as **FCP** and **TTFB**; Google’s Web Vitals guidance says the assessment should use the **75th percentile** of page loads, segmented by device class, to judge whether most users are seeing acceptable performance.[9][3]  

**CrUX field metrics to check**
- **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)** — loading speed; good when it occurs within **2.5 seconds**.[9]
- **Interaction to Next Paint (INP)** — responsiveness; good at **200 milliseconds or less**.[9]
- **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** — visual stability; good at **0.1 or less**.[9]
- **First Contentful Paint (FCP)** — first visible content paint; listed by Google as a useful loading metric alongside LCP and TTFB.[9][3]
- **Time to First Byte (TTFB)** — server response / initial network latency; listed by Google as a useful loading metric alongside FCP and LCP.[9][3]

**CrUX p75 methodology**
- Google’s Web Vitals guidance uses the **75th percentile (p75)** of page-load measurements as the target for evaluating user experience, because it reflects what *most* users experience rather than the average.[9]
- The practical testable check for launch is: **for each metric, confirm the p75 value meets the “good” threshold** for the relevant device segment.[9]
- For the Core Web Vitals assessment, all three primary metrics—**LCP**, **INP**, and **CLS**—need to be in the “good” range at the 75th percentile.[2][9]

For **RAIL**, use it as a **performance-budget model** for interaction and page lifecycle behavior: the four phases are **Response**, **Animation**, **Idle**, and **Load**, with the standard numeric targets of **respond in under 100 ms**, **keep animation work to 10 ms per frame**, **keep idle work in chunks under 50 ms**, and **load to interactive in under 5 s**.[9]  

**RAIL launch-check targets**
- **Response**: respond to user input in **< 100 ms** so the interaction feels immediate.[9]
- **Animation**: keep each frame’s work to **10 ms** or less to sustain smooth motion.[9]
- **Idle**: schedule non-urgent work in **< 50 ms** chunks so the main thread remains available.[9]
- **Load**: get the page to be interactive in **< 5 seconds** on a typical mobile connection; Google’s RAIL guidance frames this as the user-perceived loading budget.[9]

If you want, I can turn this into a **launch-audit checklist** with pass/fail criteria and a compact table for QA sign-off.
